title: Setting up a hotbed for early seed starting
subtitle: Using fresh manure as a heat source under cold frames or glass
excerpt: A hotbed uses the heat generated by fresh manure as it decomposes to warm the growing medium above. It provides bottom heat for germination and early growth without electricity. The heat lasts 4-8 weeks, enough for the main seed-starting season.
sourceNotes: Royal Horticultural Society guidance on hotbeds. Garden Organic historical hotbed methods. Composting temperature characteristics of fresh horse manure from agricultural extension research.

glossaryTerms.definition: Composting managed to reach 55-70°C through aerobic bacterial activity. A hotbed uses the same biological heat generation but harvests it for plant growing rather than waste processing. Fresh horse manure with straw reaches 60-70°C in the first week.

Fresh horse manure with straw has a C:N ratio of approximately 25-30:1, close to ideal for active decomposition. This is why it generates heat rapidly. Spent (already partially composted) manure has a higher C:N and generates less heat.
Fresh horse manure is the green (nitrogen-rich); the straw bedding is the brown (carbon-rich). The mix naturally provides a balanced starting ratio for heat generation.

content.text: Fresh horse manure with straw bedding generates intense heat as it decomposes. A layer 40 cm deep reaches 60 to 70°C in the centre within 3 to 5 days. After the initial peak, temperature settles to a stable 25 to 35°C over the following 3 to 6 weeks: ideal for germinating seeds and growing on early transplants under glass or a cold frame. The same
hot compost
biology that processes waste rapidly also makes an effective propagation heat source.
Materials
Fresh horse manure with straw: source from a local stable. The manure must be fresh (collected within the past week) and with straw bedding, not shavings. Shavings-based manure has a much higher
C:N ratio
and generates much less heat.
A cold frame or solid-sided frame: to contain the manure and support the glass or polycarbonate lid. The frame should be at least 40 cm tall to accommodate the manure layer.
Compost thermometer: essential for knowing when the initial heat has passed and the bed is safe to plant into. A standard probe thermometer with a 20-30 cm stem works well.
Building the hotbed
Step 1, fill the frame with fresh manure.
Add fresh manure to a depth of 40 cm, firming it lightly as you go. If the frame is not tall enough, build a temporary wooden side extension to contain the manure.
Step 2, cover and wait for peak heat to pass.
Cover with the glass lid immediately. The temperature will peak at 60 to 70°C within 3 to 5 days. Check with a
thermometer
daily. Do not plant into the bed until the temperature has dropped below 35°C.
Step 3, top with a growing medium.
Once temperature is stable below 35°C, add 15 to 20 cm of seed compost or loam over the manure. This is the growing layer. Sow seeds or pot on seedlings into this layer. The heat rises from below.
What to grow

A hotbed in February or March provides conditions for tomatoes, peppers, aubergines, cucumbers, and squash (all needing 18 to 25°C for germination). In a cold frame with glass, overnight temperatures inside are typically 8 to 10°C above ambient when the hotbed is generating heat. This provides frost protection through to April in most of the UK without any additional heating.

attrs.title: After the hotbed season
attrs.body: When the heat has declined and the growing season has moved outside, the spent manure in the hotbed is partially composted and makes excellent growing medium. Empty the frame and add to the compost heap or use directly as a rich mulch around established shrubs.
